---------- Forwarded message ---------
From: Eric Watson <eric.watson@highviewpower.com>
Date: Wed, Mar 3, 2021 at 5:22 AM
Subject: RE: Highview Enterprises Ltd. "Request a callback"
To: Don Gohore Be Huszar <antonio.hudnall@gmail.com>


Hi Antonio,



I’m Eric Watson, the applications engineer with Highview Power. I was
forwarded your request about a potential business opportunity.

I would like to set up a call to talk to you and understand this
opportunity more. Let me know if you have any time free next week and I
will get a call locked in.



Thank you,



*Eric Watson*
Applications Engineer

*In order to do our part to minimise the spread of the Covid19 virus, all
Highview Power offices have been temporarily closed and our employees will
be working from home until further notice. Whilst we will do our utmost to
continue business as normal, please bear in mind that response times to
enquiries and payments may be affected. We would like to strongly encourage
that all written communication be sent electronically forthwith, and any

*From:* Don Gohore Be Huszar <antonio.hudnall@gmail.com>
*Sent:* Tuesday, March 2, 2021 12:36 PM
*To:* info <info@highviewpower.com>
*Subject:* Re: Highview Enterprises Ltd. "Request a callback"



Thank you for your response.

Please find answers to your questions below.



Is your enquiry in relation to an identified project opportunity?

 No, this is not in relation on an identified project opportunity.

If so, what is the name of the project?

 The Hudn Hall Project

How big is the project (in MW and in hours duration?) Please note that we
will consider opportunities at >50MW and >4 hours

We are looking for a model that will power

 >50MW  & <24 hours

Where is it located?

Site 1 - Single Family homestead concept - Alturas Ca. USA

Site 2 - Abidjan, Cote d' Ivoire

Who is (are) the customer(s) for the output of the project?

 Single Family / Small Community

Will it be co-located with renewable energy sources such as wind or solar?
Or co-located with a source of external heat or cold? Or will it be stand
alone?

 This project was inspired to only use wind and solar.  The vision is a
stand alone complex.



Thank you,



Antonio Hudnall



On Tue, Mar 2, 2021 at 9:07 AM info <info@highviewpower.com> wrote:

Hi Antonio.



Thank you for your interest in Highview Power, a global leader in
long-duration, large-scale energy storage.



Yes you are correct, out Technology is best suited to large-scale usage,
over long periods of time.



If you have a particular project in mind please complete the below table
and return it to me. I will then forward this to our business development
team for consideration.



Highview Power is a small yet growing company; as such we need to manage
our resources carefully, and are only able to focus on projects in specific
geographies and of a specific size at this present time. It would greatly
help us to manage your enquiry if you could provide us with a little more
information.



Is your enquiry in relation to an identified project opportunity?



If so, what is the name of the project?



How big is the project (in MW and in hours duration?) Please note that we
will consider opportunities at >50MW and >4 hours



Where is it located?



Who is (are) the customer(s) for the output of the project?



Will it be co-located with renewable energy sources such as wind or solar?
Or co-located with a source of external heat or cold? Or will it be stand
alone?







Once we receive back the table above, we will endeavour to review and
respond to your enquiry as soon as humanly possible. In the meantime, you
are most welcome to sign up for *Highview Power’s newsletter!* It’s a great
way to get company news and updates and see the impact Highview Power is
having on the energy industry.

*Please sign up **here
<https://eur01.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fhighviewpower.us9.list-manage.com%2Fsubscribe%3Fu%3D90a1c82c7273f1e02fa119d9a%26id%3D39980da3bb&data=04%7C01%7C%7C2c4f5590c2cc40bbd58008d8dda1b3de%7C20ef23918e8b4f899250414e5bbd3313%7C0%7C1%7C637503033898767763%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C1000&sdata=AC%2FPgEGGmmJDBOd1OIRr9bskiLEy2lk1O8p%2BRmXan3M%3D&reserved=0>.*



Best regards,

 16′ by 16′ shed
two 350 gallon tanks
It rains just over 12 inches a year at California Pines
The roof on this shed is about 200 square feet which should collect about
100 gallons of water for every inch of rain. So in a year,
this small roof should give you 1,200 gallons of water,
which would overflow the 700 gallons of water storage pictured here.
Luckily it rains fairly steadily year-round in Modoc County
so your water supply would theoretically be used-up if you visited
your property regularly.

Rain water catcher
6 bags of concrete
(9) 4 x 4 12' long
(3) 4 x 6 12' long
(16) 2 x 6 12' long
(4) sheets Plywood
Roofing material
(2) gutters 12' long

headers.subject: Fwd: Property Value & CA Real Estate Law

emails.subject: Fwd: Property Value & CA Real Estate Law
emails.body:
---------- Forwarded message ---------
From: Patricia Hudnall <patriciahudnall@gmail.com>
Date: Wed, Oct 25, 2023 at 7:31 PM
Subject: Property Value & CA Real Estate Law
To: Don Gohore Be Huszar <antonio.hudnall@gmail.com>


I reviewed property sales activity and found the following:

   1. 500 lots sold in the last 12 months

   2. 485 lots sold in the last 6 months - June was a bang up month for
   sales. The prices were all over the place.

   3. 57 lots in the last 3 months. Generally, an appraiser will look at
   the last 90 days for comparable sales. What is most interesting is only 4
   of the 57 properties were less than one acre. These four properties, based
   on their sizing and pricing, result in the comparative properties.


What I cannot tell you is which properties, if any, have an inground well
and/or septic. This information is not included in the public record. There
is also the high probability that these facilities were not installed.

The attached Vacant Land Purchase Agreement lays out for  you the amount of
information considered in a sale. Additionally, beyond the purchase, is the
cost of inspections of both the well and septic. This cost is unknown but
I'm sure that there is a company in Alturas that performs this type of work.

In reviewing the contract, I noted that you preparing to pay the full $8500
with a credit card. If you didn't agree to this, I recommend that you
review the contract in total and ensure that it aligns with the California
Real Estate Law guidelines (attached Vacant Land Purchase Offer).

The property profile confirms the owner's name and contact information.
Let's discuss sometime tomorrow afternoon when you have time.

Sincerely,

Pat

-- 

*Patricia Hudnall,  Realtor *
CA BRE# 01934602
Investment Property Specialist
Probate & Trust Specialist
First Time Buyer Specialist
eXp Realty of California
2603 Camino Ramon #200
San Ramon, CA 94583
Email: patriciahudnall@gmail.com
Cell: 510.913.0805

---------- Forwarded message ---------
From: Don Gohore Be Huszar <antonio.hudnall@gmail.com>
Date: Tue, Oct 24, 2023 at 4:32 PM
Subject: Water Shed
To: Don Gohore Be Huszar <antonio.hudnall@gmail.com>


Islands Trust Conservancy — Overview — Islands Trust
<https://islandstrust.bc.ca/conservancy/>

POA Office
Hours of operations:
Monday - Friday: 8am - 1pm

Phone Number:
(530)233-4801

Email:
calpinespoa750@frontier.com

On-Site Administration Office:
Hours of operations:
Thursday - Monday: 8am-4pm

Ruby

Phone Number:
(530)233-5747


 16′ by 16′ shed
two 350 gallon tanks
It rains just over 12 inches a year at California Pines
The roof on this shed is about 200 square feet which should collect about
100 gallons of water for every inch of rain. So in a year,
this small roof should give you 1,200 gallons of water,
which would overflow the 700 gallons of water storage pictured here.
Luckily it rains fairly steadily year-round in Modoc County
so your water supply would theoretically be used-up if you visited
your property regularly.

Rain water catcher
6 bags of concrete
(9) 4 x 4 12' long
(3) 4 x 6 12' long
(16) 2 x 6 12' long
(4) sheets Plywood
Roofing material
(2) gutters 12' long
(2) gutter covers
PVC pipe
Debris catcher
(2) Water Tanks
(2) barrels - for filtration
Battery
Solar Panel
Solar regulator
Pump

emails.subject: Fwd: Yurt
emails.body:
---------- Forwarded message ---------
From: Don Gohore Be Huszar <antonio.hudnall@gmail.com>
Date: Thu, May 15, 2025 at 3:47 PM
Subject: Re: Yurt
To: Don Gohore Be Huszar <antonio.hudnall@gmail.com>


Residential Window Supplier San Francisco | Premium Showroom
<https://truittandwhite.com/window-door-showroom>

On Thu, May 15, 2025 at 3:46 PM Don Gohore Be Huszar <
antonio.hudnall@gmail.com> wrote:

>
>    -
>
>       For a *10-foot-long* metal stud equivalent to a *2x4 wood stud*,
>       the most common gauge options are:
>       - *18-gauge (0.0478 inches thick)* – Strong and durable, often used
>          for *structural framing*.
>          - *20-gauge (0.0359 inches thick)* – Lighter but still sturdy,
>          suitable for *non-load-bearing walls*.
>          - *16-gauge (0.0598 inches thick)* – Heavier-duty, used for *high-load
>          applications*.
>
>       If your yurt requires *structural integrity*, *18-gauge or 16-gauge*
>       would be ideal. If the walls are *non-load-bearing*, *20-gauge*
>       could work while reducing weight and cost.
>
>       Would you like help sourcing materials or comparing costs?
>
>       That sounds solid! With *9 vertical beams per wall* and *8 wall
>       sections*, you’ll have *72 beams* in total. Keeping them *16 inches
>       apart* is a standard spacing for structural stability.
>
>       To calculate your *lumber needs*, you’ll want to consider:
>       - *Beam length:* Depending on your ceiling height, each beam will
>          likely be *9–10 feet long*.
>          - *Beam material:* If you go with *wood studs*, *2x4 or 2x6*
>          beams could work. For *metal*, gauge and framing type will
>          matter.
>          - *Fasteners & Connectors:* You’ll need *brackets, screws, or
>          welding points* for stability.
>
>       Would you like help estimating how much material you’ll need, or
>       advice on sourcing?
>
>       Great! You’ll need *top and bottom plates* to secure the wall studs
>       and provide structural integrity.
>       *Materials for Top & Bottom Plates:*
>          - *Wood Option:* *2x4 or 2x6* lumber (same as your studs).
>          - *Metal Option:* *Track channels* matching your *16-gauge metal
>          studs*.
>       *Quantity Needed:*
>          - Each wall section is *12 feet wide*.
>          - You have *8 sections*, meaning *8 top plates* and *8 bottom
>          plates*.
>          - Each plate should run across the *entire length of the wall*.
>          - *Total material length:* *8 × 12 feet = 96 feet* for top
>          plates and *96 feet* for bottom plates.
>          - If using *metal track*, ensure it’s rated for *structural
>          walls*.
>
>       Would you like recommendations on sourcing materials or
>       construction techniques for assembly?
>
>       Alright, let’s break it down!
>       *Material Costs:*
>          - You need *72 metal studs* at *10 feet* each.
>          - At *$1.51 per linear foot*, each stud costs *about $15.10*.
>          - *Total cost:* *72 studs × $15.10 = $1,087.20* (before tax and
>          shipping).
>       *Weight Calculation:*
>          - A *16-gauge metal stud* weighs *~50–60 lbs per 10-ft stud*.
>          - *Total weight for 72 studs:* *72 × 50–60 lbs = 3,600–4,320 lbs*
>          .
>          - Consider *transportation*—this is about *1.8–2.2 tons*.
>
>       Would you like help finding suppliers or alternative material
>       options to optimize cost and weight?
>
>       For *roof spines* and other structural materials, here are some
>       sourcing options:
>       - *ABC Supply Co., Inc.* – A major distributor of *roofing and
>          framing materials*, with a location in *San Rafael, CA*.
>          - *Roof Observations* – A resource detailing *roofing material
>          sources* used across the U.S., including metal and wood options.
>          - *ABC Supply* – A national supplier with *over 1,000 locations*,
>          offering *roofing, siding, and framing materials*.
>
>       Would you like help comparing materials for durability and cost?
>
>
> On Thu, May 15, 2025 at 11:19 AM Don Gohore Be Huszar <
> antonio.hudnall@gmail.com> wrote:
>
>> Prefab Houses with Glass Mirror Walls – ÖÖD
>> <https://oodhouse.com/prefab/houses/>
